top_profane.text:
I've bumped into Dick Wagner more times than I can count during my musical gestation in the 70's and 80's. Whether it was wearing out the grooves of the (then) new Kiss "Destroyer" album, sitting in a darkened room with friends listening to Lou Reed as black lights fluoresced over posters by the likes of Peter Max, Rick Griffin, Keelan, and others, or pounding out carefully learned licks and chord changes from Alice Cooper's "Welcome to My Nightmare" and "Billion Dollar Babies" in any one the myriad cover bands I was a part of, he was omnipresent. So why did I title this "A fresh Ear"?

Because unlike the army of relentless self-promoters that surrounded him, each vying to take the largest piece of the spotlight, Dick was a true musician, looking to see what he could add to the lexicon that is the music of the last half century, rather than what he could take from it.

The resultant lack of hype, rather than diminishing him, diminished me, and many others like me who would have benefited by having him as an influence as we grew and learned our musical chops.

A case that illustrates this point is the album "Destroyer" by Kiss. We heard the album, but never really listened to it, after all, Ace Frehley was not a guitar player whose licks inspired us to wear out the play and rewind button on our cassette decks trying to work out the leads and change
top_profane.title: A Fresh Ear

top_profane.text:
The wait, although really not as long as it could have been, for a new album from Justin Hawkins is over. His British Whale synth pop alias was essentially a tease with just two singles released, so the anxiety over more available material has since heightened. Enter Hot Leg, Justin's new band consisting of himself and three friends. They label themselves "man rock," which is amusing but fitting (silly outfits are out, sweatbands and leggings are in). This is Hawkins' baby as he wrote, recorded, and produced everything. And what a beautiful baby it is!

Red Light Fever is arguably deserving of comparison to The Darkness' debut, [[ASIN:B0000AZKM0 Permission To Land]]. The guitar licks, fun, and flamboyancy are all there. The flavor of The Darkness goes wherever Justin Hawkins goes, and further proof exists in what his former band mates have done in Stone Gods - they just aren't the same or as popular without their departed mastermind. Hawkins is carrying Hot Leg on his back now and the band is looking to make their mark in the man rock charts.

Some songs which sound Darkness-esque, "Cocktails" and "Gay In The 80s" as two examples, are actually both co-written by Chas Bayfield, an unknown friend/writer of sorts. Was he around in The Darkness days? "Cocktails" is a very catchy song, but don't allow yourself to be caught singing the chorus aloud... `Cock [cock]
top_profane.title: This is Hawkins' baby, and what a beautiful baby it is!

The opera DIE SCHULDIGKEIT DES ERSTEN GEBOTS K. 35, is Mozart's first full-scale opera, written in 1767 in Salzburg, labeled a "cantata spirituale" only because secular operas were ruled out during the Lent season, replaced by sacred dramas. As the first part of a trilogy, it was performed on March 12, 1767, repeated on April 2, 1767.

Philips's "[[ASIN:B000050GG5 Complete Mozart Edition]]" (1991) labels it an "oratorio".
But it is a real opera, with action onstage, dramatic tension in the confrontation between two parties competing for a man's soul, and development of one character (Christian). The preaching and sermonizing is light and caricatural, and not the subject of the plot.
The 2006 Salzburg Festival included it in its production of Mozart's 22 operas. So did Deutsche Grammophon's [[ASIN:B000I8OFKU M-22 Boxset]] (2007), and Decca's CD box of "[[ASIN:B001TH28EY The Complete Operas]]" (2009).
In both Mozart's life, and the history of opera, it is an extraordinary historical achievement.

Mozart, age 11, came to this work as a full-fledged, accomplished, composer.
He already had written 6 vocal pieces, including three important arias:
- A motet in G minor K. 20, "God is Our Refuge" (July 1765, London)
- A long, 6'30", aria for tenor K. 21, "Va dal Furor portata" (London, 1765), a song of anger.
- A second substantial
top_profane.title: A stunning revelation: The most ravishing, seductive opera by the youngest composer ever

top_profane.text:
When one looks back at the alternative rock scene that dominated the first half of the 90s, Garbage's self-titled debut album immediately comes to mind as a defining album from that movement. A decade old as of this writing, "Garbage" (1995) has stood the test of time and not only contains some of the best singles from the 90s, but is a modern-day classic.

"Garbage" has many elements in its sound which can explain its broad appeal. The songs are so incredibly infectious and danceable, fans of dance-pop (Madonna, Janet Jackson) are sure to enjoy the album. But "Garbage" is undeniably a rock album, first and foremost. With crunching guitars, intense drumming, and a charismatic frontwoman, a more rock inclined audience is sure to dig "Garbage" as well. Garbage's music also encompasses an element of 70s/80s cool new-wave. In a sense, Garbage was like an updated version of Blondie for the 1990s, so fans yearning for a new Debbie Harry are sure to appreciate Garbage.

Another reason "Garbage" struck such a chord with the public is there really wasn't another band quite like them. There were bands that were precursors to Garbage, like Curve, and My Bloody Valentine, among others, but they didn't take off and explode as Garbage did, for whatever reason, probably due to marketing and timing. Garbage's debut came out at the perfect time. In an era of post-grunge, wh
top_profane.title: The classic debut

This is the real thing - 30 slabs of savage rock `n' roll. I had selections of this release on a tape many many many years ago, but it left off 10-12 numbers that I'd never heard until I finally finally got both CDs. The funny thing is that I couldn't get Use Your Illusion in any shop in Singapore, I had to ask my wife to bring them back from Japan with her. Go figure!

I love all the songs. Use Your Illusion I (gold color) starts of with "Right Next Door To Hell", not a great to song, but it does have a great bass intro, like so many of the band's best songs (a punk layover - Duff played in punk bands, which tend to enjoy using the bass to set up songs). "Dust N' Bones" is a weird, slow sleazy groaner, whiskey-soaked, really perfect, although maybe Axl does lay the experimental vocal stuff on a bit thick (some of it sounds like Tuvan throat singing - what is this, the Mekons?!?). "Live And Let Die" is the inexplicable Paul McCartney cover... well, it sounds okay, but I tend to skip over it a lot, since it is very repetitive. "Don't Cry" on the first disc is nice, sorta, but over-played (especially since we get a second version of it on disc two (I can't tell them apart, maybe need a few more listens - musically they're identical, but with different verses and vocal metre...). top_profane.text:
Just got this in 2014. I swear I have been looking for something new from Selene for years and the only thing I could find was a dead link for the Cistine song, which apparently was the only song recorded.

So I was very excited to hear this! Don't know how I missed it for four years. Some of the songs are very different from the Seven Year Bitch days, somewhat more produced than I would have liked. But when I was scanning the track listing the 2nd time, I noticed the song "Blue Side." In the waning days of Seven Year Bitch, they performed this song, including at the last three shows I saw, two in Seattle and one in Portland, in May of 1997. They played several new songs, that were to be on their next album and Blue Side was the only one I got the name of. That album never happened and it seemed those songs, which were said to have already been recorded, would never see the light of day.

I anguished, way back then, that the album which all us fans were looking forward to, never happened, and it was frustrating knowing they were sitting on a tape somewhere, never to be heard.

But after hearing it today, I believe this was the exact same version they had recorded for that record, that never happened. It really sounds like Seven Year Bitch, especially their last official album, Gato Negro.

Then I went back to Dangerous Dan
top_profane.title: Seven Year Bitch Songs On This Record!

top_profane.text:
I'm a pretty objective Madge fan here. I dig most of her stuff, but some of her musical outings have been pretty crappy to say the least.

Ray of Light (1998) as an album is hard to surpass...it was an amazing album, one where Madge basically re-claimed her musical relevance. BUT...while definitely not reaching the perfect blend of emotion, lyrical content, and heady music that Ray of Light contained (and in a slightly lesser sense, '89's Like a Prayer album)...Confessions is far better then 2003's American Life, and overall, a stronger album then 2000's Music.

I'll just got track by track, and keep it simple. I rated the tracks on a scale of 1 to 5.

Hung Up-4 stars, this song gets pretty annoying after awhile, and I think she tries too hard to be "catchy" so to speak at times...but overall, it's a strong track, and an excellent choice for a first single.

Get Together-5 stars, I hated this song when I first heard it...the sample made me think..."why must you sample Madge? Rather pathetic." But, after repeated listenings, you realize this song is rather brilliant, beautiful, and that Madge does the sample of "Music Sounds Better With You" justice.

Sorry-3 stars, rumored to be the second single...probably the catchiest track off the album, but after some time, it loses its magic in my opinion. I dig Madge saying "sorry"
top_profane.title: Not her best...but nowhere near her worst.

top_profane.text:
I am a Sting ultra-mega-superfan. Have been for well over 30 years. The track by track review that follows hurts to write.
1- crap
2- crap
3- crap
4- crap
5- crap
6- crap
7- FANTASTIC!
8- crap
9- Great!
10- Very nice

It should be noted, the tracks I enjoyed just so happened to be the ones that Sting wrote himself or co-wrote with ANYONE OTHER THAN HIS BAND!!!

These favored tracks sharply contrast with the others in that they are not overproduced, not loud for loudness' sake, and not full of trite lyrics and the neo new wave jazz-pop chords that Dominic Miller seems to bring. (Don't misunderstand that to mean I dismiss Mr. Miller's talent. I don't at all. I just don't like his writing influence on Sting's melodies.)

One of Sting's many strengths I have enjoyed the most has been his ability to craft quiet introspection into an engaging lyric ("Consider Me Gone," "Fragile," Why Should I Cry For You?," "Fields of Gold," "Hounds of Winter," "A Thousand Years," etc.). It feels like Sting was stuck in a frat party and playing along so as not to be a party pooper (tracks 1-6), went outside for a smoke break (7), walked his way back through the frat house (8) and into his bedroom (9-10) where he passed out.

One can only hope he has a quiet day to himself to really gather his thoughts.
top_profane.title: A fandom of diminishing returns

top_profane.text:
Two things are amazing with DICK HAYMES!

1) That this christmas cd is, as of this writing, discontinued.
2) That DICK HAYMES, to my knowledge, never recorded an actual christmas album in one session (perhaps spread over a couple of days). This is such a crazy thing it might be considered criminal!

What DICK HAYMES has done is record several christmas songs individually throughout the years and this cd is a compilation of them. The songs seem to all be from the 1940's to the early 1950's and so they carry that very old 'nostalgic' christmas sound to them reminding one of the old christmas recordings of BING CROSBY, PERRY COMO, and FRANK SINATRA. And believe me, DICK HAYMES is as ever great a singer as these three gentlemen who gave us some of our best christmas music. That's why I find it so wrong that DICK HAYMES never recorded a christmas album in one session like the others did. It boggles the mind.

Every song is great here and as DICK HAYMES sings them as great or greater than anybody, in general, at least. There are several different backing orchestra's and chorus' on this cd since the songs are from scattered recordings throughout the years. But everything compliments DICK HAYMES tot he finest.

LET IT SNOW and the two versions of WHITE CHRISTMAS are standouts here as are I'VE GOT MY LOVE TO KEEP ME WARM and IT CAME UPON A MI
top_profane.title: Nostalgic Christmas At It's Best

I don't know why His Master's Voice made this recording in the first place. After all Menuhin had made a stereo recording of Beethoven's Violin Concerto in 1960 with the Vienna Philharmonic under Constantin Silvestri, in excellent stereo sound and a publication which had been received with accolades in The Gramophone ([[ASIN:B00005UUOE Beethoven: Violin Concerto / Romances No. 1 & 2]]). So why re-do it six years later? And I think, in those pre-Naxos years, they even PAID the performers that they recorded, not just royalties on sales, but upfront, and hefty sums too when they were stars like these. Maybe EMI (the owning company of HMV) was hoping to bank on two of their greatest stars and on the universal success of Klemperer's complete Beethoven symphonies, recorded in the late 1950s to early 1960s. And Klemp' had never recorded Beethoven's Violin Concerto (he would do the complete Piano Concertos a year later with Barenboim - and there are no recordings left of him, studio or live, in the Triple Concerto).

If so, it misfired. The recording was generally received with more than reluctance, and to this day it appears to be always dismissed in favor of Menuhin's earlier versions, especially those with Furtwängler, the more famous one with the Philharmonia Orchestra from 1953 ([[ASIN:B000002SG3 Beethoven, Mendelssohn: Violin Concertos (Violin Concerto in D Major,
top_rant.title: much better than its reputation (me against the critics). MP3 BUYERS, BEWARE!

I was never a real `fan' of musicals until I saw Funny Face. Even then, it took a second viewing for it to really sink in. While not as exuberant as Singin' in the Rain or as grandiose as My Fair Lady, it holds a special place in Audrey Hepburn's heart as one of the most enjoyable performances of her career--and it shows. Audrey only had to play herself to portray Jo, the pearl trapped inside the shell of self-doubt. In many ways, this mirrored her real life at the time. Reflecting her desire for something light after the grueling War & Peace of 1955, the film is bursting at the seams with energy throughout.

Sound quality-wise, the recording does leave a little to be desired. Though not pulled directly from the film as some may indicate, but rather the recording sessions that make up the film's soundtrack, it is identical to what you'll hear while watching it. This would be a prime candidate for a remaster.

On to the tracks! If you're reading this review, you have probably seen the film, but I feel like writing a bit for each one as a reminder.

1) Overture: Funny Face/'S Wonderful/Think Pink

top_rant.text: Well, I started with The Unguided's 2011 debut entitled [[ASIN:B005VMHB7M Hell Frost]] (see my review) and have worked my way back through the history of the people in both bands (for those who don't know, Richard, Roger, and Roland were in this band and then left and eventually created The Unguided). I listened to [[ASIN:B001BMWNME Love & Other Disasters]] (and reviewed it) and it was an amazing album. This album is more of their great mix of melodic death metal and metalcore, but it's not as well done as the other two. It came before the other 2, however it's like a mix of them; The Unguided's Hell Frost is much heavier than S.S.'s Love... and this one is in the middle. However, it doesn't have the diversity of either, namely Love and Other Disasters. This album follows a pattern on nearly every song (harsh verses and clean choruses) and it's only broken by Enclave, which is an amazing song! The band is still doing well on this album though. Roger and Robin provide some excellent guitar riffs, John gives us some solid beats, Karin gives some good bass lines, and Richard & Roland show up every other metalcore band how vocals are supposed to be done. The DVD that is included with this Tour Edition is cool. It has some music videos and other stuff...but the main reason I got the DVD was because a. I wanted the bonus track on the album and b. There's a concert on the DVD. The con

top_rant.text: Yes, this album is great progressive metal and yes it's better than whatever progressive metal you have heard before! This band is its own. You really can't compare their music to anyone else's because Evergrey is truly unique! There is no other band in the world that sounds like this. No other band makes music so complex, yet so emotional. No other band has 5 guys playing music this heavy that is this beautiful! Tom S. Englund is one of the greatest vocalists in metal today. He is also one heck of a guitar player! Henrik Danhage is the other guitar player on this album and these two make an EXTRAORDINARY duo! This band is from Sweden and they manage to capture that Gothenburg Melodic Death Metal Guitar tone, but they add a new flavor and flair to it. I'd compare these two to Jesper and Björn of In Flames, but Tom and Henrik are on a whole other level of playing. That's right, Jesper and Björn can't match Evergrey! And to be honest, no guitar duo is on their level! Not Li/Totman of DragonForce, not Friedman/Mustaine of Megadeth, not even Smith/Murray of Iron Maiden match this level! The closest guitar duo in my mind is Sjunnesson/Johansson of The Unguided and they're still way over on left field, not to mention that they just made their first album in 2011 (10 years after the release of this Evergrey album!)!. Michael Håkansson is an awesome bassist when he can be heard. Sven K
top_rant.title: The Greatest Progressive Metal You've Never Heard of!

top_rant.text: ...because it's Journey (one of the best rock bands of all time), it honestly isn't bad, but it's way below Journey's usual standards, and because I feel like rating it a 3 would be too harsh. Ok, so you can tell just from that first sentence and the title that this doesn't rank as a great Journey album. But it isn't bad. Contrary to belief, however, Trial by Fire is MUCH better than this album, so anyone that says otherwise hasn't really listened to TbF. Now, the lineup on this album is the very talented Steve Perry on vocals, who delivers the usual amazing performance. Neal Schon has some moments on here, but the absence of great leads and hooks from the guitar is  BLATANT and is really what makes this album weak! This album is too stripped down from the rock that we got on Frontiers and even on Escape! Jonathan Cain is a good keyboardist, but he has WAY too much presence on this album. His key parts act as a lead and they make the music sound weaker because a guitar should have played those leads. That is the only official lineup on here. The studio musicians satisfy in some spots and drastically add to the weakness on others. Steve Smith is on 3 songs and that's good because I love his drumming, but sadly, 2 of those songs are so weak, that I don't even care that he's drumming on them! Bob Glaub does the bass on the tracks with Smith and he's not bad, but he's not Ross Valo

ROBIN TROWER WILL ALWAYS BE THE QUINTESSENTIAL GUITARIST OF PROCOL HARUM. TROWER ALWAYS WEAVES HIS MELODIES INTO THE CONTEXT OF THE MATERIAL. This is a review for the deluxe edition of the album, and I would suggest that everyone buys the deluxe version, it is definitely worth it for the bonus tracks.

Let me begin this review by stating that this is the best Judas Priest record since 'Painkiller'. The one small gripe that stops it from getting a perfect score from me is the shoddy production that muffles Scott Travis' double bass (especially noticeable on the song 'Metalizer'). However, when playing the physical CD copy of the album on a good stereo system, the production isn't bad enough to disrupt the listening experience.

To the performance of each individual on this album;

Rob Halford:

Despite what some would have you believe, Rob's vocals are not weak on this album. In fact, for mine he is simply nailing his mid range vocals and, thankfully, he sticks to this mid range for much of the record.

His highs on this record are hit and miss, from great on 'Halls of Valhalla' to not-so-great on 'Metalizer' and with “Battle Cry' we get a bit of both. Luckily, he keeps the shrieking to a minimum on this record and it really helps his performance.

For a singer on the north side of 60, his performance is stellar.

Glenn Tipton:

top_five_star_obscene.text:
J. D. Simo has figured it out. A perfectly wonderful guitarist/singer/songwriter, Simo did the smart thing, and managed to hook up with a rhythm section which is his equal, and they make what I call 'a glorious noise.'

SIMO is their debut album, and it's a very auspicious birth. It's going to get called things like retro, '60s, this that and the other, and deservedly so, but it's really just great rock, played by a cool bunch. Together a little more than a year, they've been unleashing their hard rock fury over audiences, and garnering a great deal of attention along the way.

Straight out of the box they evoke the halcyon tones of the best of the sixties - Aoh is a driving rock and roller that barrels along like a freight train. Produced by bassist Frank Swart at Fry Pharmacy in Nashville, and he got it just right. It's raw as raw gets, and the mix is great - the rhythm section is front and center, leaving Simo to weave his magic over the top like a surfer riding a torpedo. He manages to stay on top, and it attests to his power as a singer and player. Swart and drummer Adam Abrashoff are a hyperkinetic coupling who never back down. They take Simo's Yardbirdsian intro and go off into an experiential ride down the best side of another era. I can throw all kinds of cliches at this, and they wear them very well. These fellas play like they mean it - no preten
top_five_star_obscene.title: SIMO - A Glorious Noise

top_five_star_obscene.text:
A new Cock Sparrer CD is here after ten long years, and not only does it sound great, it tips well and can be applied directly to warts. I hesitate calling them the first Oi band as it's not my fight, and I mostly agree with the assessment Sparrer and others were proto-Oi bands. Sparrer have been going at it for thirty odd years, tower over their peers as musicians, and in Here We Stand have recorded the best album of their career.

The only complaint I have with Here We Stand is how the tracks were ordered (I just got word the American release will address this issue). Sparrer deliver three types of songs: thoughtful laments on what once was and is either no more or has gone wrong, funny tales of corner pub numbnuttery, and positive anthems of pride. They write catchy tunes in their sleep, as do The Buzzcocks. You somehow expect more than that from Cock Sparrer. I graded eight of the fourteen tracks eight. Here We Stand is front-loaded with eights that hit similar themes. The best tracks, and many of their best don't take themselves seriously, don't kick in until the middle. The best are "Spirit Of '76", "Last Orders", "Suicide Girls", "Sussed", "Don't Stop", "Despite All This" and "Too Late".

With Cock Sparrer you can always rely on simple melodies improved upon to the point of excellence, rollicking bass guitar, soaring guitar chords and clever single no
top_five_star_obscene.title: Best Sparrer Album Ever

top_five_star_obscene.text:
When we were kids Nick scared the baby crap out of us with Birthday Party. We grew up with him and weren't quite as scared because The Bad Seeds' stories were night-time fables told by a benevolent campfire master. Just leave the light on, we'll be OK even if dreaming of being buried under ten feet of snow white snow.
Now we're all grown up. Middle age, actually and Nick's still right here in the room. Maybe he's exorcised his personal demons. Maybe he hopes we have too. Yeah right.
Grinderman wakes us from the complacency of midlife. We stopped bitching and fighting and we started whining. Like old men on the porch. "In my day there were fights at every show." Blah,blah,blah.
But Nick reminds us that we're still pissed off. And it's OK to be pissed. Maybe we've slowed down. But we're not dead. Yet. And neither is Nick.
When we were 21 we had to juggle girls like Naomi Watts juggling for King Kong. OOOps, dropped a few. Now, after break-ups and divorces and rehabs the woman don't seem as ethereal, sweet and horny. Do they? We've got the "No P-ssy Blues" and thank God Nick is still here with us to commiserate. Play it over and over on the way home from the party. Loud. And don't forget to feed the cat before you go to bed. Alone. Thanks Nick for sticking around.
top_five_star_obscene.title: Life IS Pain

top_five_star_obscene.text: Everyone on here who says " oh buy this album instead cause these guys suck " is crap. 1981-82 was not only busy years for the Whispers, but they were great years. This music makes me feel ashamed of being born in the early 80s and growing up to most of the crap thats out in black music. The team kept the formula going by givin' us something to dance to and romance to, but only this time each album side seperated the type of songs.

Every track is good and its good enough to have a single on its own, but the late Dick Griffey was'nt havin' that (lol). "In the Raw" is funky messenger groove (#4 on the R&B charts) about "being yourself." What great Whispers' groove is not complete is without a short rap verse from the Scott brothers (Hip-Hop has caught on to them as it recently had for The Sylvers, Stevie Wonder, and Teena Marie). "Turn Me Out" and "Emergency" (#22 on the R&B charts) were groovy, yet galactic tunes that work up quite a sweat, thanks once again to Leon Sylvers III and some of his siblings and members of Dynasty who wrote and played background instruments on all the dance songs on the album. Although it appears that many of those songs Leon put together sounds like much of the SOLAR roster, The Whispers have their own melodies that work with these songs and the song writers knew what they were doing.

top_five_star_obscene.text:
In 1990, Madonna had released her sixth album entitled, I'M BREATHLESS. This album, not a soundtrack to the film, "Dick Tracy", but contains music from and inspired the film, is simply great. It most certainly does not disappoint.

HE'S A MAN
A great way to start off this "Dick Tracy" inspired album. If you listen closely to the very start of the song, you'll a clip from the film, "Dick Tracy"

SOONER OR LATER
This song was performed by Madonna twice in the film. She performs an unplugged version (not this version) in the beginning before "Big Boy Caprice" (played by Al Pacino in his most annoying role yet) captures Lips Manlus. The second time she performs this song (this version) is when "Big Boy Caprice" has his grand opening at Lips Manlus' club, after taking over the club. Great jazzy song. Even my father likes this one.

HANKY PANKY
The album's second & final single. Without a music video to support it, this song hit the #10 spot (Billboard Hot 100). I had the pleasure of seeing Madonna perform this classic on RE-INVENTION TOUR in 2004, and she did an awesome job of it.

I'M GOING BANANAS
Love this tune.

CRY BABY
Not bad.

SOMETHING TO REMEMBER
A beautifully performed ballad. Expect to see this tune to pop as the title track to Madonna's 1995 greatest ballads album entitled, SOMETHI
top_five_star_obscene.title: Madonna ... I'M BREATHLESS (1990)

top_five_star_obscene.text:
There's not a guitarist out there who can play Jimi Hendrix's stuff better than the man himself - so what does Robert Dick think he's doing? Well, first of all, Mr Dick doesn't play the guitar. He plays the flute. And believe me, this ain't your maiden Aunt Mildred's pipe, (unless she was hanging around with John Cage).

Robert Dick is probably the best, and certainly the most adventurous flutist in jazz today, (or yesterday for that matter). On "Third Stone From the Sun," Mr Dick takes on Mr Hendrix's "Pali Gap," "Purple Haze," "Voodoo Child" and, of course, "Third Stone...," as well as three originals. He plays the flute, alto flute, bass flute, F contrabass flute and an A flat piccolo. He overdubs, plays chords, uses circular breathing - flutters and wails - and generally tears up the landscape. He is more than adequately assisted by Jerome Harris on various guitars, Jim Black on drums, the Soldier String Quartet and, on "Third Stone...," Marty Ehrlich on bass clarinet.

New World Records has done an excellent job on the sonics: Clean and precise, with just a hint of warmth.

"Third Stone..." is exciting, cutting edge jazz - but it is not for the faint of heart.
top_five_star_obscene.title: Not For the Faint of Heart

top_five_star_obscene.text: This collection of Dick van Dyke's favorite songs will definately make you "Put on a Happy Face!"  The songs on this album include NICE WORK IF YOU CAN GET IT* EASY STREET* PUT ON A HAPPY FACE* WALKING AND WHISTLIN' BLUES* BABY WON'T YOU PLESE COME HOME* ANY PLACE I HANG MY HAT IS HOME* AIN'T WE GOT FUN* THEY ALL LAUGHED* WIVES AND LOVERS* LAZYBONES* MY BABY JUST CARES FOR ME* WHEN YOU WANT 'EM, YOU CAN'T GET 'EM WHEN YOU'VE GOT 'EM, YOU DON'T WANT 'EM* and I'VE GOT A CRUSH ON YOU. The original album came out in 1963 around the same time he was doing the movie version of Bye Bye Birdie.  The original title was "Songs I Like by Dick van Dyke."  Dick van Dyke is a very talented man and a great singer and that is shown very well in his only full solo album.  It's hard to find, but very much worth the search.  If you like Dick van Dyke's singing, you should check out the original Broadway cast recording and motion picture soundtrack of Bye Bye Birdie.  His other musical credits include the soundtracks to Mary Poppins and Chitty Chitty Bang Bang. Another intersting musical venture of his was the 45rpm recod he put out in the late 50's.  It has two songs on it: THREE WHEELS ON MY WAGON and ONE PART DOG, NINE PARTS CAT. It is more for the sake of comedy than music, but its pretty neat to have.  All in all, I love this Cd ("Put on a Happy Face"), and just about everthing else he's done

top_five_star_obscene.text:
Unlike the earlier releases on Atlantic (Stay Hard and The Pack is Back), Raven had complete control over the production of Life's a Bitch. There were no record execs pressuring them to sound commercial and "sell, sell, sell". No heavy-handed producer was brought in to take over the project and make them use synthesizers and horn sections (ala The Pack is Back). In fact, Atlantic had all but given up on Raven and pretty much let them do whatever they wanted on their final Atlantic release. The result was quite possibly the best Raven album of their career.

Start to finish, Life's a Bitch shows Raven in peak form. The songs are loud, insane, and catchy. The guitar solos are larger than life. The arrangements are fresh and varied. You have 2-and-half minute slam-fests like "You're a Liar" and epic prog-like rockers like "On the Wings of an Eagle". Of course the usual Raven lunacy is on proud display in "Overload", "Pick Your Window", "Life's a Bitch", and "Playing with the Razor". There honestly is not a weak track on here. No filler, no shortcuts, no disposable over-produced yawners. This is Raven doing penance for the earlier Atlantic releases, which quite frankly really weren't their fault.

You can imagine being young starving musicians, coming to a new country, with a new record deal, and your bosses telling you what to do. Raven was at the mercy of Atla
top_five_star_obscene.title: This is Raven done right

There are many reviews that go into great depth and I agree with many...especially some of the comments made by "Cowpunk1". I started listening to The Damned in about 83 or so via tape cassettes from friends. My first purchases of the Damned was in 1985, which consisted of the "Black Album" and "Another Great Album" (best of). At the time I lived in H.B. and was into the hardcore scene but was hopelessly infected by the Damned and their sound, which I became addicted to. Their sound ranges from catchy hooky rhythms to haunting theatrical sounds.

I hate to make this comparison, but the Damned remind me of the Beatles...don't freak out...give me a chance. I am an Elvis (sun records era) man not a Beatles fan, but the Beatles were talented and through out their career experimented and changed styles with great artistic and popular success.

I like the original sound of the Damned as well as Grave Disorder and all the albums in between. They truly showed what talent coupled with real artistic creativity can accomplish...30 years of a wide range of styles and sounds all of which are "bitichin".

They say the "Black Album" is an experimental album delving into the psychedelic...uh ok..probably..It is a great album and I recommend it highly. If I were allowed only 10 albums/cds to listen to for the rest of my life, 3 would be by the Damned (Machin
top_five_star_obscene.title: A band on a journey...
